... Lascaris’s Erotemata, a Greek–Latin grammar, for roughly twice
as much as he paid for the Hypnerotomachia two years later.
Goodhart was able to buy the book at a reduced price because it is defective.
It is missing the first four leaves and the last four leaves, corresponding to the
first gathering and the last gathering. The first gathering contains the front
matter regarding Leonardo Grassi’s sponsorship of the book, and its absence
may not have been noticed by an earlier owner since the next gathering begins
with a half-title page and proceeds into the introductory text of the...

... from that of Tertullian and 
Aristides, in that he had little interest in "messenger" dreams, in which the 
gods would communicate something of personal, social or religious impor-
tance to the dreamer. His lack of interest in divine dreams makes sense, since 
many of these dreams did not require the services of an interpreter—and 
Artemidorus was a dream interpreter by profession! He does speak of dreams 
as "god-sent" but explains that he uses this terminology in a general way, 
just as people commonly call unexpected events "god-sent." He purposely 
avoids the question of the...

The alternative appellation 'essay' is an anachron- 
ism, for the word was not coined until the time of 
Montaigne (1580). Sir Francis Bacon ( Works, ii, London 
1837, P. 44) distinguishes the treatise from the essay in 
terms of length, detail and organization: 'To write just 
treatises, requireth leisure in the writer, and leisure in 
the reader ... which is the cause that hath made me 
choose to write certain brief notes, set down rather 
significantly [i.e. suggestively] than curiously [i.e. with 
careful art], which I have called "Essays"... 
that is,

region IIB, where architecture and the landscape in general become vehicles 
for metaphorical meaning and serve a crucial edificatory function, the 
buildings in region IIA, which are not described, and the immaterial space in 
region IIC serve mostly as the containers of the action and acquire ritual 
significance through the movements of the characters in and out of them.

The preceding studies of the ‘Encyclopedic’ annotators documented readers who used the 
process of annotating the HP for the twofold purpose of gathering  material for the rhetorical 
canon of inventio, and the refinement of their own ingegno.  The HP served as both a 
resource for composition, and a means of  sharpening one’s capacity to apply that 
information.  The copy of the 1545 Sons of Aldus edition at shelfmark C.60.o.12 in the...

obvious Veneto geographic location, indeed given the evidence it is impossible to say the author could not have

But is Francesco Colonna, the friar of SS. Giovanni e Paolo, born 1433, the author? There are issues with 
this attribution which require consideration. Firstly, Colonna venezia, who was not ordained until the age of 30, did 
not receive his bachelors degree until 40 at the university of Padua, perhaps displaying a slow acquisition of 
grammar, arithmetic, logic, rhetoric, music, and astronomy, in the Dominican curriculum....

and requires] with strange shapes and veils with ambiguity the true meaning of the information be

Thresholds pertaining to the main narrative cannot be crossed at will, but require a form of 
interior advancement by Poliphilo, preventing narrative progression until he is adequately 
prepared. Progressing beyond the narrative thresholds requires two elements: the desire for Polia, 
and a need to achieve something in himself to progress to her. In the first realm defined...

... important narrative areas bearing a cosmological significance 
that is absent in the Roman de la rose, where allegory is related to an ascending ontology, pursuing 
the Apuleian model.67 This highlights how space is determined through walking as an allegory of 
self-transformation: as Poliphilo requires no further transformation, the narrative requires no 
further walking, thus the smaller garden is determined through the physical stasis in the narrative.

... to him when he was travelling through 
Thessaly as a university student. Here is his story in brief: 
Thelyphron comes across a man making a public announcement offering to pay a large 
sum of money to anyone who will guard a corpse overnight. Since he has spent all of 
his money, Thelyphron inquires about the job. He learns that someone is required to 
watch the dead body because witches are known to take pieces off the faces of corpses 
for their magical concoctions. What makes the job especially difficult is that the witches 
can transform themselves into animals or insects in order...

Julius II (HP Godwin, p. 244 [p6v], Gombrich 1951, p. 120). He 
also suggested changing the orientation of St. Peter’s Basilica to be in 
harmonious relationship with the Vatican obelisk, which, as 
Gombrich claims, he considered a religious symbol, a conception he 
must have acquired from HP (Gombrich 1951, p. 121).
